next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Minjae's half-latched workshop door was visible from the alley mouth behind the radiator market, a strip of dark metal that meant hurry and also get out if anything looked wrong. Ina stayed under a dead pharmacy sign and watched the shuttered pawn window across the block until the reflected umbrellas stopped changing. Only then did she make a second lap and cut back in through rain hard enough to flatten cigarette butts into cracked concrete. Water spilled from broken gutters, hit rusted awnings, and came down again in colder drops that found the back of her neck and ran inside her shirt. The city smelled like wet asphalt, fryer oil, and hot wire insulation. Somewhere above her a compressor kicked on, coughed, and held a thin mechanical whine that made everything underneath it feel temporary.

She reached the workshop door and knocked once with her ring finger. He opened before the sound finished.

"You're late," he said. "Or you got tailed and took your time deciding whether I should die with you."

He wore a black sleeveless undershirt darkened with sweat across the chest. His left shoulder, where an old scar cut diagonally toward his collarbone, shone with flux smudges. He had a soldering iron in one hand and a paper cup of instant coffee in the other. It was nearly midnight and he was still standing like he'd just started his shift.

"If I got tailed, I wouldn't come to your door," Ina said.

"People always think that until they're scared enough." He shut the door, threw the dead bolt, and pointed at a plastic stool by the workbench. "Phone. Battery out."...
open_hooks_to_carry: Servers and scripts are seized during the raid.
Interrogators present contract evidence and threaten espionage charges.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...For the first time all evening, he looked tired.

"Necessary witnesses," he said.

She left before she said something that would have burned options she still needed.

The stairwell smelled like wet dust and old glue. On the second landing she set the folder on a radiator pipe, unlocked her phone, and opened the mitigation console that had appeared after her signature cleared.

Payload `v3_2_mixA` was still queued in tonight's northbound batch for clusters C-17 and D-04.

She selected EMERGENCY RETIRE.

A warning box filled the screen: `Propagation to active couriers begins on confirmation. Rollback unavailable after acknowledgment.`

Her thumb was slick with rain. She wiped it on her shirt and pressed confirm.

Thirty seconds later the phone rang.

"Writer Seo?" an on-call dispatcher said over engine noise. "I got your kill order. Two bikes are already at the Uijeongbu handoff. If I pull now, they eat the penalty and every broker asks who burned the run."

"Log it under my credential," Ina said. "Pull them anyway. Push family-support advance to both couriers out of my discretionary line."

Silence, then the dispatcher exhaled. "Understood. Route seven rerouted. Hash v3_2 is dead as of 19:42."

Outside, rain slapped the street hard enough to bounce. Delivery scooters hissed past, plastic ponchos snapping. Ina tasted blood where she had bitten the inside of her cheek without noticing.

She forwarded the reroute confirmation to the new mitigation inbox, sent Minjae a message asking for raw logs within the hour, and headed back toward Studio C before the crossing light turned.
state_deltas_required: Protect collaborators and avoid prosecution during an intelligence sweep.
Ina accepts a controlled-informant arrangement, creating a sustained double-bind.
